speaker: interviewer
text: OK. My name is Myra Slotnick, and it is my pleasure to be interviewing Henry Kronberg in Las Vegas, Nevada on December 26, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: How about '95?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we do that again? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Myra, milk?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you believe that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Myra? Milk?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, thank-- no, thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Plain black coffee?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not too strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Myra Slotnick, and it is my pleasure to be interviewing Henry Kronberg in Las Vegas, Nevada on December 26, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Can you please tell me your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Henry Kronberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell your last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-R-O-N-B-E-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it was Heinrich Kronberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No nicknames.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birth date?

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age today?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the city and the country in which you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Germany, on the east part of Germany, Breslau.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell me the name of your mother and your father? My father's name was Aaron. And my, my mother's name was Anne. And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bila.

speaker: interviewer
text: And spell that for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: B-I-L-A, Bila.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have sisters and brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Esther, Esther Kronberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her married name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her married name-- her last name was Powell. And she had a nickname. Her name was Lala.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And everybody know her as Lala.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the Jewish community in which you grew up in the town where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The town I was born, we didn't spend our time there, because my father was traveling. And we-- most of our-- most of my life I spent in the city of Katowice. That was on the Polish side of the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a border town, actually between Germany and Poland. And there was a border town. The name of the, the town was Katowice. It's K-A-T-O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how-- before the war, how long did you live in that town, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the war was-- broke out in 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your father-- how did your father make a living in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was a watchmaker. And he had a little watchmaker shop, a little jewelry watchmaker shop in Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, how did you-- can you describe your Jewish life for me in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish life-- we were quite very close together. I start my grade school in a Jewish, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish grade school. And it was only for Jewish kids in the, in that city. And we were very close. And when I was 10, 11 years old, I joined a Zionist organization, by name, Ha-No'ar ha-Ziyyoni. So we had a very nice Jewish life and Jewish community. We were--  there was a big Jewish temple over there. Quite-- there was a Jewish community around that big temple. So we were close together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my mother had a little millinery shop.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the same town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the same town, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe a typical Passover for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I cannot describe a very typical Passover, because we were not too religious. We did have a Passover dinner. Naturally, everybody else had. But my family was not-- my father was not too religious. He was Jewish. And my mother, she was Jewish, too, but we were more or less assimilated Jews. We were not very religious Jews.  We used to go-- I used to drink with the-- I used to go to the synagogue. Used to go quite often, but we were not the devoted Jews. Let's put it that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you describe the, the relationship between the Jewish community that you lived in and the non-Jewish community in your town before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, it was not too great. There was a big anti-Semitic [INAUDIBLE] in in the, in the town where we used to live, because, actually, the school was segregated. We, we went to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish school, so we were, like, segregated. We had-- the principal was non-Jewish guy, but all the teachers were Jewish. But we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] occasionally we had some fights with the non-Jewish kids, which maybe was normal. But we used to get along. Maybe not very well, but we [LAUGHS] used to get along.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father have customers that were both Jewish and non-Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same way. Same way. No, that-- there was no, not, there was no problem where there was a segregated customers or something like that. But then, naturally, in the school, we used to have some fights occasionally, now and then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the subject of the fights because of your religious affiliation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, definitely. They used to call us Jews or something like that and make fun of us or something like that. So actually, we picked up fights.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you say was the-- one of the turning points in your life at the-- when the Nazis--

speaker: interviewee
text: My turning point-- we had a nice life. I [LAUGHS] felt that everything was OK. We had-- everything was comfortable. We had a-- we weren't rich, but we were not-- we were comfortable. When the war broke out-- at that time, my whole life changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you give me one example that you remember at just the time when the Nazis occupied your town of a change?

speaker: interviewee
text: We fled our city where we used to live because we knew that it was a border town. And we fled. I went with my father. And my mother went with my sister. And we fled the, the city because we knew that in case-- when the war broke out, the city where we used to live was going to be right at the beginning of the front. So we just went deeper to the east, like 20 miles, 25 miles away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where, where would that be?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: S-O-S-N-O-W-I-C-E.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in what country?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also Poland. That was Poland, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how, how long did you stay in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that town, I stayed with my father for about three, four months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever hear about where your mother and sister went?

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew where they went, but that was the last time I saw my mother, when we separated, because she went to friends of ours in a-- further away to a different city. And I never seen my mother again after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me the circumstances under which you decided, or your family decided, to split up as opposed to staying together?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the circumstances was, it was much easier to travel. And we wanted [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go later on-- we had plans to meet my-- meet up with my mother and my sister, but that was impossible, because we couldn't travel. There was a restriction of traveling right after that and for Jews especially. You could not leave the city. And we were restricted only to, to the city where we were. And right away, they made all kind of restriction. And we just stayed where we were, and we couldn't go out there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know the circumstances under which your mother and sister were living at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I found out later on that my sister went to Russia, because right after the war broke out with, between Germany and Russia, she went to Russia, where she lived through in Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever find out exactly what happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not exactly. But I got the news that some people who knew something about her, that she was killed in the city where, when they're, where liquidating the Jewish population of that city, that she got killed in that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and what city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was Borisoff.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was more to the Russian border on the-- it was still on the Polish side.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your father and you in the city that you went to? How long did you stay there? What were your living conditions?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed for around three, four months. And my father decided-- he had some jewelry with him, and he tried to make a, a living. That's how we-- he couldn't work. So we tried to go to a larger city, you know, in order to maybe sell some of his belongings. And he went to city of Cracow-- 'Cracoff' Polish-- in 'Cracoff'. But it's Cracow. And he was supposed to come back, but he never came back. After three, four weeks I didn't hear from him, so I decide-- I was smuggling myself on the train that time, and I decided to go to the same city. And I found out that, unfortunately, he was picked up on a raid, because the Germans used to make raids on the Jewish sections. Certain sections they made the raids, and they went from house to house, and they used to confiscate a lot of things. And, and they caught my father, also on that raid, with that jewelry. They confiscate the jewelry. And they, they locked him in, in a, in a prison. And that's how I found out that he was in a prison. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I tried to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] do all my best in order to release him from the prison, but I was not successful. And one day-- my father was a very-- his hobby used to be listen to operas. He had a radio and listen to it. He was-- he liked music. And one day, early in the morning, I went in front of the prison, and I knew that one of those windows were facing the street. And I couldn't holler in order for him to, to, to see me, but I whistled his favorite tune. And was very quiet. And he came over to the window. And I had a chance to, to, to say a few words to him. And fortunately, after two weeks later, they released him from the prison. And we lived in Cracow for few months until every, all the Jews had to go to, go to ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, they made all the Jewish population-- all the Jewish people had to go to Jewish ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow ghetto. Cracow ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the conditions of that ghetto for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was very cramped, very cramped. I can imagine that-- I don't know how many thousands of Jews used to live in a big sections, and all of a sudden the whole ghetto was maybe [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] five or six blocks versus a, a half a city, because Cracow had a big Jewish population. It was a big Jewish population. I don't know exactly how many thousands of Jews were there, but there was-- I don't know if it was 50,000 or 70,000. But it was very cramped. I don't remember how many thousand people. But it was very cramped conditions. Fortunately, for me and my father that I'm-- on one occasion, where we had to-- it was before we went to ghetto-- we had to register to-- every Jewish person had to register, to go to [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] labor, labor office, like. And the Germans used to come, come to the labor office, and they used to request so many Jewish workers for their purpose, whatever purpose it was, to clean the facility, to wash or to carry coal or to carry furniture or whatever it was. And one time I was picked up by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the German Gestapo headquarters, whoever it was. And we worked on the Gestapo headquarters as a laborer. I was carrying coal. I was carrying furniture because the Gestapo were in charge of liquidating and confiscating all the wealth or whatever they were taking away from everybody, and mostly Jewish population. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I become one of the workers. And because I became a useful person-- when the ghetto started in Cracow, they designated one building for their workers. And at that headquarters, there was a lot of other profession, like-- not profession, I mean, carpenters, mechanics, car mechanics, and shoemakers, tailors, and so, so on. So there was over 140 people [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who were servicing that headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that-- after that, I met somebody over there who was from my hometown. And instead of being a block laborer, he took me to help him to be a painter, to paint. And I become a painter. I learn how to paint, and I become a painter. So I had a little-- it was not so hard for me. It was much, much easier work by being a painter than carrying coal from the cellar to the fourth or fifth floor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was my fortunate. And maybe because I worked for them and was a useful Jew-- that's what they used to call it-- so maybe my fate was-- look, look a little bit [INAUDIBLE]. And I was-- fortunately later on when they liquidated the ghetto, I was fortunate to save my father.

speaker: interviewee
text: But coming back to the situation when working for the headquarters-- when I become a painter, my job was painting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] walls, officers' halls, whatever, in that, in that headquarters. That headquarters was like three blocks, two blocks square. So there was a lot of work to do there. And part of my job was painting the walls after they beat up a lot of [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] people, which they were-- not-- interrogating in the Gestapo headquarters. And they used to beat those poor people to, to, to the-- sometimes to death. And the blood used to splatter on the wall. So they used to call me to paint over the, the walls. Now that was part of my job. And I see a lot of tragedy at that headquarters. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we used to go, go from the ghetto, used to walk every morning to the headquarters. We used to work from 7 o'clock in the morning. We had to be there at 7:00 in the morning until 6:00, 7:00 at night. And we walk back home every, every day. And that, that lasted for almost a year. Once, before we were going home, they told us to meet, that, that we should assemble in one hall, all of the workers that-- they told us to assemble in a hall. Once we came in-- the Gestapo headquarters had a gate. And once you were in, you could not get out, if you were not allowed to. So they assembled us in the headquarters, I mean, in the, in the, in the hall. And they gave us the news that we not going back.

speaker: interviewer
text: All 140 people are-- and some people had wives and children living in the ghetto. And they told us that we not going back home anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were afraid. What, what's going to happen? Are they-- and we thought, are they going to maybe shoot us? But they didn't shoot us. They locked-- they [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] packed us on trucks, and they drove us to prison.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Gestapo was in charge of all the prisons in, in Cracow. There was a famous prison in, in Cracow named Montelupich. There was a very old prison, and very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like with dungeons and everything. It was a very bad prison. And they locked us up in that prison. And they told us that we not going to go anymore back home to ghetto. And they will house us in that prison. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And every morning, they load us on the trucks, and we were going to the headquarters to do the work. And at night, they brought us back to prison. You can imagine that it was a very dramatic thing, because I was single. I had my father. But between us, between those 140 people, there was a lot of people what they had families, wives, kids, children, in the, in the ghettos. And all of a sudden, they were not allowed to see the children or their wives back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we didn't know what's going to happen, but the rumors were that we-- that they're going to liquidate the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still in that ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was still in the ghetto. And because I knew a lot of [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Gestapo officers over there, I plead with one captain [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that my father is a watchmaker, and he's a jeweler, and maybe he will be able to be useful for the, for the headquarters to fix people's watches or-- because they needed people to work. And somehow I persuaded [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one of the majors over there to give an order that before they liquidated the ghetto, they should bring my father to the prison. Actually, the prison saved our life. Because we were locked up in the prison, we were, we were safe to-- in the prison. That saved our lives. At that time they liquidated the ghetto, literally. They took a lot of the people from the ghetto to concentration camps. The one who was older, they used to, they used to shoot, shot, shoot a lot of people, kill them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They went to hospitals, and they liquidated the hospitals. They used to shoot the people in bed. My father was telling me, when he came in-- they found him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because he was in the house where the-- actually was designated for us, so they-- the, the major gave a order to look for my father. And they found him. And they put him on a truck. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They put him on a truck. And the same truck stopped in a nursery, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and they took about 20 kids from the nursery, on the same truck. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they stopped. They, they drove over to Plaszow. Plaszow was a concentration camp not far from, from the Cracow ghetto. And was in the That's where it was. They took the kids. They stopped at, at the field by the concentration, concentration camp. They took the kids out, and my father was left in the truck. And he heard when they shot all those 20 kids. When my father came in to prison, when I met him, he was in shock. He couldn't stand it. He says he, he was, he was beside himself. And he told me that. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he was-- it was terrible. It was terrible. And that's how they liquidated the ghetto.
